# Data from Grid Strategies reports

# 2017 values from 2018 report / 2021 report / 2022 report
# 2024 values from the 2024 Grid Strategies report Figure 2

data = {
    'ERCOT': {'2017': 976, '2024': 1750},  # 2024 ~$1.75B
    'ISO-NE': {'2017': 41, '2024': 306},   # 2024 ~$306M
    'MISO': {'2017': 1518, '2024': 1800},  # 2024 ~$1.8B
    'NYISO': {'2017': 481, '2024': 1900},  # 2024 ~$1.9B
    'PJM': {'2017': 698, '2024': 734},     # 2024 ~$734M
    'SPP': {'2017': 500, '2024': 37},      # 2024 ~$37M
}

print("ISO/RTO\t\t2017 ($M)\t2024 ($M)\t% Change")
print("-" * 65)
for iso, values in data.items():
    v2017 = values['2017']
    v2024 = values['2024']
    pct_change = ((v2024 - v2017) / v2017) * 100
    print(f"{iso}\t\t${v2017:,}\t\t${v2024:,}\t\t{pct_change:+.1f}%")

print("\nHighest percentage change:")
changes = {iso: ((values['2024'] - values['2017']) / values['2017']) * 100 for iso, values in data.items()}
highest = max(changes, key=changes.get)
print(f"{highest}: {changes[highest]:+.1f}%")

These elevated costs demonstrate how inadequate transmission capacity raises power prices for customers, driven fundamentally by limited transmission expansion and exacerbated by rising electricity demand and extreme weather. These costs persist even as technologies like battery storage have stepped up to relieve congestion in certain regions. The grid’s inability to freely move power remains a structural barrier that imposes substantial costs on consumers and impedes the reliability that comes with access to a more diverse generation portfolio.\n\n1 While congestion costs are generally only transparent within regions with an organized wholesale market, meaning those with a regional transmission organization (RTO) or independent system operator (ISO), to estimate a national congestion cost figure that includes non-RTO/ISO regions, this report scales the known RTO/ISO congestion costs in Table B1 in the Appendix according to the peak load of the same regions when compared to total U.S. load.\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nFIGURE 1. Transmission congestion costs by RTO/ISO, as reported by the internal and independent market monitors, 2024\n\n2 Note that electricity congestion costs spiked in 2022, largely due to surging natural gas prices caused by the onset of the war in Ukraine.\n\n$1.8 B $37 M\n\n$1.8 B\n\nMISO\n\nSPPNon-RTO West\n\nNon-RTO Southeast\n\nNYISO\n\nPJM\n\nCAISO\n\nERCOT\n\nISONE $734 M $1.9 B $1.75 B $306 M\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nAbout transmission congestion\n\nWhen the grid cannot deliver the lowest-cost generation to load, more expensive generation is dispatched instead, raising prices. This cost is reported as “transmission congestion” in organized wholesale markets administered by RTOs/ISOs. While in some cases these costs can be hedged through financial instruments, they provide a measure of how inadequate transmission capacity raises power prices for customers.\n\nGrid congestion costs reveal where the system is constrained and can signal the need to build additional transmission infrastructure.\n\nNote that the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) requires transmission planners to conduct a robust evaluation of potential transmission projects, using multiple measures of the benefits of new transmission, before a transmission planner can move a project forward. One of those metrics is the value of new transmission for relieving costly congestion. This report focuses on congestion rents, which represent the difference in market prices between two locations multiplied by the amount of power flowing between them. Congestion rents are the most common— and often the only—metric reported by RTO/ISO market monitors for quantifying and comparing congestion across the grid. import pandas as pd

data = {
    'ISO/RTO': ['ERCOT','ISO-NE','MISO','NYISO','PJM','SPP','CAISO'],
    '2017': [976,41,1518,481,698,500,138],
    '2024': [1900,37,1800,306,1754,1800,734]
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)
df['Change ($M)'] = df['2024'] - df['2017']
df['% Change'] = ((df['2024'] - df['2017']) / df['2017'] * 100).round(1)
print(df.sort_values('% Change', ascending=False))

      "text": "5 Congestion Revenue Rights | Market Performance Report\n\n# 5 Congestion Revenue Rights\n\nCongestion Revenue Rights are a financial tool developed for the purpose of allowing load and exports to hedge against congestion charges paid when congestion occurs on a transmission line (aka congestion rents as was described in the previous section). If the congestion occurs in the same direction the CRR is held for the CRR holder is paid, but the CRR holder may have an obligation to pay if congestion occurs in the opposite direction.\n\nCongestion revenue rights auction efficiency 1B became effective on January 1, 2019. It includes key changes related to the congestion revenue rights settlements process:\n\n- California ISO Reports\n- Targeted reduction of congestion revenue rights payouts on a constraint by constraint basis.\n- Distribute congestion revenues to the extent that CAISO collected the requisite revenue on the constraint over the month. That is, implement a pro-rata funding for CRRs.\n- Allow surpluses on one constraint in one hour to offset deficits on the same constraint in another hour over the course of the month.\n- Only distribute surpluses to congestion revenue rights if the surplus is collected on a constraint that the congestion revenue right accrued a deficit, and only up to the full target payment value of the congestion revenue right.\n- Distribute remaining surplus revenue at the end of the month, which are associated with constraints that collect more surplus over the month than deficits, to measured demand.\n\n## Monthly CRR Revenue\n\nFigure 11 illustrates the daily CRR notional value in the corresponding month for the various transmission elements that experienced congestion during the month.       "text": "Congestion Revenue Rights Auction \nEfficiency \nTrack 1B Draft Final Proposal Second Addendum \nJune 11, 2018 \n\nPrepared by: Market and Infrastructure Policy California Independent System Operator\n\nCAISO/M&IP/Perry Servedio 2 \nTable of Contents \n1 Executive Summary ... 3 \n2 Changes to this proposal ... 5 \n3 Scope of this proposal ... 6 \n4 Initiative background ... 8 \n4.1 Initiative organization and status ... 8 \n4.1.1 Initiative organization ... 8 \n4.1.2 Status ... 8 \n4.2 Stakeholder engagement ... 10 \n4.2.1 Energy Imbalance Market Governing Body ... 10 \n4.2.2 Schedule ... 10 \n4.2.3 Summary of stakeholder comments ... 10 \n5 Proposal background ... 14 \n5.1 General discussion ... 14 \n5.2 Congestion revenue rights ... 15 \n5.3 Auction efficiency ... 16 \n5.4 Specifics of the CAISO congestion revenue rights processes ... 18 \n5.4.1 Annual process ... 18 \n5.4.2 Monthly process ... 21 \n5.5 Certain aspects of other ISO/RTO financial transmission rights markets ... 23 \n6 Proposals and alternatives considered ... 25 \n6.1 General discussion ... 26 \n6.2 Proposal ... 29 \n6.2.1 Reduce congestion revenue rights payments based on effectiveness on constraints ... 29 \n6.2.2 Lower the percentage of system capacity available in the annual allocation and auction..34 \n6.3 Alternatives considered ... 36 \n6.3.1 Reduce congestion revenue rights quantities each day prior to the day-ahead market ... 36 \n6.3.2 Eliminating release of available transmission system capacity in the auction ... 37 \n7 Next Steps ... 41 \nAppendix ... 42 \n\n## 1 Executive Summary\n\nThe CAISO proposes to change the congestion revenue shortfall allocation to equitably \nallocate shortfalls among congestion revenue rights and eliminate incentives to bid for \nlow-priced high-payout paths. The CAISO proposes to do this by reducing congestion \nrevenue right payments, so as to not exceed the congestion revenue collected in each \nday-ahead market, based on each congestion revenue right’s settled flow on market \nconstraints generating congestion revenue right payment shortfalls. The CAISO also \nproposes to reduce the percentage of system capacity available in the annual allocation \nand auction process to more accurately model the transmission that is ultimately \navailable in the day-ahead markets. \nThis document also describes two other alternatives the CAISO considered: (1) \nreducing congestion revenue right quantities each day prior to the day-ahead market so \nthat they reflect available transmission, and (2) not releasing any transmission capacity \nin the congestion revenue rights auction so that bids would only clear if there was a \ncorresponding bid in the opposite direction. \nSince 2014, market participants purchased congestion revenue rights in the auction for \nan average of $99.5 million per year less than their eventual payouts (termed “auction \nrevenue shortfall” in this document). On average, market participants purchase \ncongestion revenue rights for 63 cents on the dollar. When day-ahead congestion \ncharges are insufficient to cover the difference, it is allocated as uplift to load serving \nentities. \nThese auction prices are likely inefficient because the auction prices are substantially \nbelow the congestion revenue right payouts based on day-ahead market congestion. \nAuctioned congestion revenue rights are primarily intended for hedging congestion \nassociated with supply delivery in the CAISO’s locational marginal price-based day \nahead market. If congestion revenue rights were priced on this basis, then congestion \nrevenue rights auction prices would, at least over the long-term, be more reflective of \nactual day-ahead market congestion revenues.1 \nTrack 1A of this initiative addressed low auction prices with changes intended to make \nthe auction more competitive through concentrating congestion revenue right bidding \nactivity by restricting eligible node pairs in the auction. Track 1A also partially \naddressed unforeseen transmission outages by requiring additional outage information \nprior to the annual congestion revenue right allocation and auction process. The CAISO \nfiled these proposed changes with the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) \non April 11, 2018 and FERC is currently considering them in FERC Docket No. ER18- \n1344. \n\n1 As adjusted for CAISO charges and the time value of money.\n\nThis Track 1B proposal further addresses high payouts to congestion revenue rights \nthat are due to modeling differences between the auction and the day-ahead markets, \nresulting in day-ahead market congestion revenue shortfalls. \nThe CAISO targets the June 2018 Board of Governors’ meeting for policies developed \nin this Track 1B proposal so that they can be in effect by this year’s annual congestion \nrevenue right allocation and auction process. \n\n## 2 Changes to this proposal\n\nThis section describes the changes the CAISO made to its draft final proposal \naddendum in this second addendum. \nIn this second addendum, the CAISO proposes to add a safeguard to mitigate the risk \nthat its constraint-specific proposal could pose to any specific congestion revenue \nrights. During its June 7, 2018 Market Surveillance Committee meeting, the Market \nSurveillance Committee expressed the concern that due to the CAISO’s large amount of \ncongestion revenue right revenue inadequacy, any specific congestion revenue right’s \npayments had the potential to be significantly de-valued. The Market Surveillance \nCommittee was concerned that this could diminish the value of congestion revenue \nrights and could particularly adversely affect market participants with smaller congestion \nrevenue right portfolios. Consequently, the Market Surveillance Committee \nrecommended to socialize the revenue inadequacy costs by allocating them to all \ncongestion revenue rights holders in proportion to their congestion revenue right \npayments. \nStakeholders at the meeting expressed concerns with this approach and, in general, \npreferred the CAISO’s proposed constraint-specific revenue inadequacy cost-allocation \napproach. They stated that, in valuing congestion revenue rights, they could better \nestimate the risk of transmission outages than they could estimate their potential share \nof congestion revenue right revenue inadequacy. Stakeholders also stated that the \nCAISO’s constraint-specific approach would better reduce incentives to obtain \ncongestion revenue rights that have inflated payments due to modeling differences \nbetween the auction and the day-ahead market. Finally, they stated allocating revenue \ninadequacy in proportion to congestion revenue right payments would inequitably affect \ncongestion revenue rights purchased at a higher price relative to their payments more \nthan it would affect congestion revenue rights purchased at a lower price. \nNevertheless, to address the Market Surveillance Committee’s concern while still \nimplementing its constraint-specific revenue inadequacy cost allocation approach, the \nCAISO also proposes to reduce the amount of system capacity released in the annual \nallocation and auction process to 65%. This additional measure is intended to dampen \nany extreme congestion revenue rights de-valuations by only releasing congestion \nrevenue rights that are reasonably expected to be feasible. As discussed in prior \nversions of this proposal, the CAISO found that by limiting the release of system \ncapacity to 65%, it can eliminate 57% of monthly transmission infeasibilities. This \nmeasure should decrease the likelihood that a congestion revenue right obtained in the \nannual process will have its payment reduced below its target payment.       "title": "Memorandum",
      "author": "Hildebrandt, Eric;Kurlinski, Ryan;Avalos, Roger;Swadley, Adam",
      "text": "CAISO/ E. Hildebrandt Page 1 of 5\n\n## Memorandum\n\nTo: ISO Board of Governors \nFrom: Eric Hildebrandt, Executive Director, Market Monitoring \nDate: August 24, 2022 \nRe: Department of Market Monitoring report \n\nThis memorandum does not require Board action.\n\nEXECUTIVE SUMMARY \nThis memo provides an update by the Department of Market Monitoring (DMM) on two \nforms of congestion related costs that increased significantly in the second quarter of 2022. \nBoth of these costs have exceeded totals for all of 2021 in just the first half of 2022. \n Real-time congestion offset costs increased from $34 million in the second \nquarter of 2021 to an estimated $120 million in the second quarter of 2022. \nCongestion offset costs in the first half of 2022 have totaled $150 million, which \nexceeds the $146 million in congestion offset costs incurred in all of 2021. \n Ratepayer losses from the congestion revenue rights auction were almost $58 \nmillion in the second quarter. This brings total ratepayer losses to about $70 \nmillion in just the first half 2022, compared to $43 million in all of 2021. These were \nthe largest losses since the ISO instituted significant changes to the auction starting \nin the 2019 settlement year. \nAdditional details of these issues are provided in this memo and in DMM’s forthcoming \nquarterly and annual reports on market issues and performance. \nREAL-TIME CONGESTION OFFSET COSTS \nThe real-time imbalance offset cost is the difference between the total money paid out by the \nISO and the total money collected by the ISO for energy settled in the real-time energy \nmarkets (i.e. the 15-minute and 5-minute market). Within the ISO system, the real-time \nimbalance offset costs are allocated as an uplift to measured demand (i.e. physical load plus \nexports). \nThe real-time imbalance offset charge consists of three components corresponding to the \nmain components of real-time settlement prices: (1) energy, (2) congestion, and \n(3) transmission losses. Any revenue imbalance from the energy components of real-time \nsettlement prices is collected through the real-time imbalance energy offset charge (RTIEO). \nRevenue imbalance from the congestion component is recovered through the real-time \ncongestion imbalance offset charge (RTCIO). Revenue imbalances from the loss \n\ncomponent is relatively small and is collected through the real-time loss imbalance offset\n\ncharge.\n\nCongestion offset costs are caused by a difference between the market model used in one market run for a given time interval and the market model used in a subsequent market run for the same time interval.       "text": "5 Congestion Revenue Rights | Market Performance Report\n\n# 5 Congestion Revenue Rights\n\nCongestion Revenue Rights are a financial tool developed for the purpose of allowing load and exports to hedge against congestion charges paid when congestion occurs on a transmission line (aka congestion rents as was described in the previous section). If the congestion occurs in the same direction the CRR is held for the CRR holder is paid, but the CRR holder may have an obligation to pay if congestion occurs in the opposite direction.\n\nCongestion revenue rights auction efficiency 1B became effective on January 1, 2019. It includes key changes related to the congestion revenue rights settlements process:\n\n- California ISO Reports\n- Targeted reduction of congestion revenue rights payouts on a constraint by constraint basis.\n- Distribute congestion revenues to the extent that CAISO collected the requisite revenue on the constraint over the month.
